informal_statement.text: Given two integer slices a and b, return the length of the longest sequence of integers that appears as a (not necessarily contiguous) subsequence of both a and b, in the same relative order.
preconditions: a and b are slices of i32 values of any length
required_properties: the result is between 0 and min(a.len(), b.len()) inclusive
there exists a sequence of indices i_1 < i_2 < ... < i_k into a and j_1 < j_2 < ... < j_k into b with a[i_m] == b[j_m] for each m and k equal to the result
no strictly longer such common subsequence exists
edge_cases: a and b share no elements (result is 0)
one input is a subsequence of the other
repeated elements within a single input

semantic_units.description: Range bound: the result is in [0, min(len(a), len(b))].
common_failure_modes: stating only an upper bound of min(len(a), len(b))

semantic_units.description: Existential witness: there exist strictly increasing index sequences into a and b of equal length k pointing to equal values.
common_failure_modes: omitting the strict-increase condition on indices
comparing values element-wise across slices of different lengths

semantic_units.description: Maximality: no common subsequence strictly longer than the returned length exists.
common_failure_modes: confusing maximality with uniqueness of witness

semantic_units.description: Contiguous vs subsequence distinction: the definition allows non-contiguous index selections.
common_failure_modes: solving the longest common substring instead of subsequence

semantic_units.description: Symmetry: lcsLength a b = lcsLength b a.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Arr), lcsLength a b ≤ a.length ∧ lcsLength a b ≤ b.length
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The result is bounded above by the lengths of both inputs.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Arr), ∃ (ia ib : List Nat), CommonSubseq a b ia ib ∧ ia.length = lcsLength a b
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: There exists a common subsequence of length equal to the returned value.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Arr) (ia ib : List Nat), CommonSubseq a b ia ib → ia.length ≤ lcsLength a b
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: No common subsequence is strictly longer than the returned value.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Arr), lcsLength a b = lcsLength b a
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: LCS length is symmetric in its arguments.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem spec_lcs_length (a b : List Int) :
  let r := lcsLength a b
  r = Nat.findGreatest (fun n =>
    ∃ (s : List Int),
      s.length = n ∧
      IsSubsequence s a ∧
      IsSubsequence s b) (by
        -- n is bounded by min lengths
        simp [Nat.findGreatest, List.length])
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The function returns the maximum length n such that there exists a list s of length n that is a subsequence of both a and b (i.e., the LCS length).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLength_def (a b : List Int) :
  lcsLength a b = (lcsDP a b).result
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned value is exactly the result extracted from the dynamic-programming table/structure used by the implementation.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
def IsSubsequence (s t : List Int) : Prop :=
  ∃ (u v : List Int), t = u ++ s ++ v ∧ s = filter? -- (or an equivalent inductive definition)

-- obligation: the implementation uses the same notion

theorem IsSubsequence_iff_impl (s a b : List Int) :
  IsSubsequence s a ↔ implIsSubsequence s a ∧ IsSubsequence s b ↔ implIsSubsequence s b
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The subsequence relation used in the specification matches the one implicitly relied upon by the implementation (e.g., via an inductive definition or an equivalent predicate).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem dp_invariant (a b : List Int) (i j : Nat) :
  i ≤ a.length → j ≤ b.length →
  (lcsDP a b).dp i j =
    Nat.findGreatest (fun n =>
      ∃ (s : List Int),
        s.length = n ∧
        IsSubsequence s (a.take i) ∧
        IsSubsequence s (b.take j)) (by
          -- boundedness by min lengths
          simp [Nat.findGreatest])
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: For every DP cell (i,j), the stored value equals the LCS length for prefixes a[0..i) and b[0..j).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem dp_recurrence (a b : List Int) (i j : Nat) :
  i > 0 → j > 0 →
  let ai := a.get? (i-1)
  let bj := b.get? (j-1)
  match ai, bj with
  | some x, some y =>
      if x = y then
        (lcsDP a b).dp i j = (lcsDP a b).dp (i-1) (j-1) + 1
      else
        (lcsDP a b).dp i j = Nat.max ((lcsDP a b).dp (i-1) j) ((lcsDP a b).dp i (j-1))
  | _, _ => True
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The implementation’s DP transition satisfies the standard LCS recurrence: match case adds 1 from (i-1,j-1), mismatch case takes max of (i-1,j) and (i,j-1).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem dp_base_cases (a b : List Int) :
  (lcsDP a b).dp 0 0 = 0 ∧
  (∀ i, (lcsDP a b).dp i 0 = 0) ∧
  (∀ j, (lcsDP a b).dp 0 j = 0)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: DP initialization is correct: LCS length is 0 when either prefix is empty.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLength_le_min (a b : List Int) :
  lcsLength a b ≤ Nat.min a.length b.length
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The computed LCS length never exceeds the length of either input (since any common subsequence cannot be longer than the shorter list).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLength_sound (a b : List Int) :
  ∃ (s : List Int),
    s.length = lcsLength a b ∧
    IsSubsequence s a ∧
    IsSubsequence s b
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: There exists an actual common subsequence of both a and b whose length equals the returned value (not just an upper bound).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLength_optimal (a b : List Int) :
  ∀ (s : List Int),
    IsSubsequence s a →
    IsSubsequence s b →
    s.length ≤ lcsLength a b
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Any common subsequence s of a and b has length at most the returned value (so the returned value is optimal).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsDP_terminates (a b : List Int) :
  ∃ res, lcsDP a b = res
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The DP computation is well-defined/terminates for all finite input slices (totality of the implementation).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem dp_index_safety (a b : List Int) :
  ∀ i j, i ≤ a.length → j ≤ b.length →
    (lcsDP a b).dp i j is defined and any internal array access is within bounds
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: All array/table accesses performed by the implementation are within bounds for the given prefix indices.
